title: Roast beef sirloin
subtitle: Salt-rubbed, rested, and served pink
excerpt: A joint of sirloin roasted hot and fast, rested until the juices settle, and carved in thick slices. The centrepiece of a British Sunday lunch.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Roast beef entries, temperatures and resting times.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Roast beef method and basting guidance.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Salt the joint up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate uncovered. Bring to room temperature for an hour before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven; reduce to 180 after the initial blast

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving cooked meat in a warm place before carving, allowing the muscle fibres to relax and the juices to redistribute so the meat stays moist when cut.
Cooking the surface of meat at very high heat to develop a brown crust, adding colour and flavour.

content.text: Roast sirloin is the joint that earns the name. It runs along the back of the animal above the ribs: well-exercised enough to have flavour, tender enough to eat pink. A 1.5 kg boneless sirloin feeds six and cooks in just over an hour.

The two critical things are a probe thermometer and patience. Pull the joint at 52 degrees C for rare to medium-rare, then rest it for at least 30 minutes. Cut into it early and the juices run across the board.
What you need
A roasting rack holds the joint above the pan juices so the underside roasts rather than braises. A V-shaped rack works well for a rolled joint.
Method
Prepare the joint

Take the beef out of the fridge an hour before cooking. Pat it dry with kitchen paper. Rub all over with the softened butter, then press in the salt and pepper. If using mustard powder, mix it into the butter first.
Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Scatter the onion, carrot, and celery across the base of the roasting pan. Set the rack on top and place the joint fat-side up on the rack.
Roast and probe
Roast at 220 degrees C for 20 minutes to
sear
the surface and colour the crust, then reduce to 180 degrees C. Start checking the internal temperature after another 40 minutes.

Pull the joint when the probe reads 52 degrees C at the thickest part for rare to medium-rare. For medium, wait for 58 degrees C; for well done, 68 degrees C. The temperature rises a few more degrees during resting.
Rest and carve
Transfer the joint to a warm plate and tent loosely with foil.
Rest
for at least 30 minutes, up to 45 for a joint this size.
Carve across the grain into slices about 1 cm thick. The trivet vegetables and pan juices are the base of a red wine gravy.

items.symptom: The joint is grey throughout, not pink
items.cause: Overcooked, or the internal temperature climbed too high during resting.
items.fix: Use a probe thermometer every time. Pull at 52 degrees C for rare to medium-rare. A 30-minute rest raises the core by about 3 to 5 degrees C, so factor that in.

items.cause: The joint was not rested long enough.
items.fix: The minimum rest is 30 minutes; 45 is better for a joint this size. Keep the foil tent on.

items.symptom: The surface is pale rather than browned
items.cause: The oven was not hot enough at the start, or the joint surface was wet.
items.fix: Make sure the oven is fully up to temperature. Pat the surface completely dry before buttering.

items.symptom: Probe readings vary across the joint
items.cause: Uneven thickness, or the probe is touching a fat seam rather than the muscle.
items.fix: Push the probe into the thickest part of the meat. Take two or three readings and go by the highest.

content.text: Variations

A rub of fresh horseradish mixed into the butter is a good alternative to mustard powder. Bone-in sirloin needs a slightly longer roast and a lower temperature because the bone conducts heat unevenly.
Where this dish lives

The British Sunday roast is built around beef. Every cookbook from Mrs Beeton onward devotes pages to it, arguing over basting schedules and dripping versus butter. The sirloin is the prestige cut: more expensive than topside, leaner than rib, and well-suited to eating pink. It reached its current form in nineteenth-century domestic cooking and has not changed much since.
